learning_outcomes.description: understand the principles of self-regulation, co-regulation and statutory regulation in the Australian media and communication industries;
recognise the application of laws relating to intellectual property, privacy, national security and defamation to the media and communication industries in Australia and other jurisdictions;
critically evaluate the application of ethics, regulations, and the law to specific circumstances and cases in the media and communication industries, particularly in Australia;
act ethically as a practitioner in journalism and professional communication.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment will present a case study describing an ethical dilemma faced by someone working in media or communications — for example, a journalist, advertiser, public relations practitioner, or digital media content producer. The person at the centre of the case must navigate competing professional, personal, and institutional pressures in the course of their work.

The case will involve a situation in which the person faces a decision, whose handling carries significant consequences for a range of stakeholders. These stakeholders may include the person themselves, their sources or clients, their employer or organisation, the audiences or publics they serve, and the wider community.

The full text of the case study will be distributed on Blackboard in Week 3. 

Please read the case carefully and then write a 1200-word response (excluding references and the AI table you need to append) that addresses the questions below.


Questions

  • What conflicting interests, and about which stakeholders, does the case present?
  • What core ethical principles and professional values are at stake in this case, and how are they reflected in the relevant codes of ethics (for example, those governing journalism, advertising, public relations, or digital media practice)? Where do they come into conflict?
  • How would each of utilitarianism, deontology, and virtue ethics be used to guide the decision at the heart of the case?
  • If you were in this situation, what would you decide to do? Select one of the ethical approaches from question #3 to explain your choice.


Guidance on Sources

In addressing these questions, you are free to use the recommended readings assigned for this course. However, do not rely extensively on the course materials. You will also need to support your arguments with further literature on ethical theories, media and communications ethics, and professional practice. You can use as many resources as you need. This may include academic literature, real-life examples of how practitioners have dealt with similar dilemmas in the past, and relevant reports (such as the influence of advertising or commercial interests on editorial or creative content). You should cite all the resources that you use in your answers, including materials from the recommended course readings. Use APA Style for citations and reference lists.


Assessment Criteria

The criteria sheet is available on course Blackboard page. 


Use of AI and Machine Translation

AI Policy for Assessment #2: Every instance of AI or MT usage must be clearly referenced, and a completed AI Info Table, accessible on Blackboard, should be attached to summarize their use of AI at the conclusion of the assessment. Students not using AI should use the AI Table to indicate that they have not used AI.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Product/ Design, Role play/ Simulation
assessments.task_description:
Background

In a significant move that captured global attention, Australia became the first country in the world to legislate a ban on social media access for users under 16. The Online Safety Amendment (popularly known as the Social Media Ban) Act took effect in December 2025, placing the onus on platforms to take "reasonable steps" to prevent under-16s from holding accounts, with penalties of up to A$49.5 million for platforms that fail to comply. The restriction applies to major platforms including but not limited to Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, Threads, TikTok, Twitch, X, YouTube, Kick and Reddit.

The law represents a significant shift in how governments approach youth safety online, moving beyond self-regulatory frameworks toward direct statutory intervention. More than six months on, its effects are being closely watched around the world — and Australia is no longer alone, as a growing number of countries are considering or advancing their own age-based restrictions. This makes Australia an early test case in an emerging global regulatory trend.


Your Task

Working as a reporter who specialises in media and technology, produce an evidence-based analytical piece that evaluates Australia's social media ban now that it has been in force for more than six months. Your piece should assess the extent to which the ban has worked (or not worked), why, what challenges have emerged, and what consequences — intended and unintended — it has had. Your analysis should also situate the Australian law within the wider international trend.

Your piece should address the following:

  • Stakeholder impact and consequences. Evaluate the effects of the ban through the perspectives of different stakeholders, such as parents, young people, platforms, educators and policy makers — recognising where their interests align and conflict. Consider the implementation challenges and the intended and unintended consequences for these groups and for the wider media market.
  • International comparison. Situate the Australian law within the broader global trend of regulating young people's access to social media. Comparing it with measures adopted or proposed in other countries, where does Australia's approach differ, and where does it overlap?
  • Lessons. Drawing on the above, what lessons should regulators — in Australia and elsewhere — take from Australia's experience so far?


Format and Length

You may submit your piece in whichever format best suits your strengths and the story you want to tell. Options include, but are not limited to, a written news feature or article, a podcast, a newscast, an interactive blog/websites, or a documentary.

  • If you submit a written piece, it should be 1200 words (including any introduction and conclusion, excluding references, appendices and the AI Table to be appended as part of the assessment submission file).
  • If you submit a multimedia piece (such as a video or podcast), each minute of multimedia is equivalent to 120 words. A 1200-word equivalent is therefore approximately 10 minutes of finished multimedia asset.


Whichever format you choose, your submission should demonstrate critical thinking, engagement with multiple perspectives, and careful consideration of practical implications, and should be written or presented in an appropriate professional style. Your piece must be grounded in research: you are expected to engage with relevant literature on the effects of social media on young people, methods for protecting children online, and the consequences of statutory regulation and content-access restrictions. This research should draw on both academic sources (such as peer-reviewed journal articles) and professional and media commentary (such as industry reports, expert analysis, and credible journalism). You should cite all the resources that you use, including any interview material and materials from the recommended course readings. You are free to use any citation style that you see fit with your delivery style.


Optional: Interviews 

Because the law has now been in effect for some months, you have the opportunity to gather your own primary material. You are encouraged — though not required — to interview relevant stakeholders such as parents, young people, educators, platform representatives, or policy experts to enrich your analysis. For example, if you are doing a podcast or a video, you can include short clips to illustrate your points, or in a news article, you can quote material from your interviews. Original interview material is not mandatory, but well-chosen interviews can add depth and authenticity to your piece. Where you draw on interviews, identify your sources appropriately and obtain their consent. Since this is an assessment, if you choose to do interviews, please do not publish the contents of the interviews elsewhere (including on social media).


Assessment Criteria

The criteria sheet is available on course Blackboard page. 


Use of AI and Machine Translation

AI Policy for Assessment #2: Every instance of AI or MT usage must be clearly referenced, and a completed AI Info Table, accessible on Blackboard, should be attached to summarize their use of AI at the conclusion of the assessment. Students not using AI should use the AI Table to indicate that they have not used AI.

assessments.task_description:
!!! Please read the description for Assessment #4 before reading this description for Assessment #3, it will help you better understand the requirements of the task.

For Assessment #3, you will present your initial ideas for Assessment #4 training material in a 3-minute lightning presentation (you cannot exceed 3 minutes).

The presentation format is precise: You will have 9 slides in total, including your title slide, and each slide should be displayed for exactly 20 seconds before automatically advancing.

The purpose of this presentation is to pitch your initial ideas for the training material. It should answer the following questions:

  1. Which law domain (e.g., privacy, intellectual property) and which jurisdiction (e.g., Australia, EU, China) will your training material will focus on?
  2. What is the profile of the employee that you are trying to train? Also think about what kind of a media organisation you are simulating. These two will help you answer the next question.
  3. What do they need to know about the law domain? What are the key and relevant provisions of the law? You need to make sure that you are covering the aspects of the law that are most relevant to the profile of the employee and the type of media company you selected.


Your lightning talk must be a concise, compelling, and well-structured pitch. You will be evaluated based on your idea and the quality of your presentation.

Given the strict time limit, you should plan the content for each of your 9 slides carefully and rehearse it extensively.

Following you presentation, there will be a 3-minute Q&A. The Q&A will focus on your decisions and your factual knowledge of the law domain and associated ethical principles.


Assessment #3 is a hurdle for Assessment #4. Assessment #3 operates as a hurdle for Assessment #4. Students who do not achieve a passing grade for Assessment #3 will receive a final grade for Assessment #4 of zero marks. 

What if you cannot present at your scheduled time? Students who miss or fail Assessment #3 will be allowed to retake this assessment before submitting Assessment #4. Please contact your course coordinator, Lemi Baruh, immediately. You will be allowed to retake the assessment before submitting Assessment #4. A separate time will be scheduled as an office hour for you to present your pitch.


Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment

  • All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)
  •  Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.

Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of:

  • Moderation of marking;
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.


Assessment Criteria

The criteria sheet is available on course Blackboard page.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The presentations will take place during Week 11 and Week 12 tutorials.

assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Media Law Training - Final Training Material
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Product/ Design, Role play/ Simulation
assessments.task_description:
You are working as a training coordinator of the Human Resources department at a media company. Start by choosing the type of media company (e.g., an online news source, social media platform, newspaper, an AI video company) that you are working at. The Chief Learning Officer has given you the task of developing training materials regarding relevant laws.

These training materials should serve as a resource for training new staff members in understanding and complying with these laws and maintaining ethical standards. As a pilot for his training program, you need to develop the training materials for one of the areas listed below. Please choose a domain that is relevant to the company you are working for (e.g., whistle-blower protection for a news organisation). This may include the following; however, you may choose another relevant domain with the approval of the course coordinator:

  • Data privacy
  • Freedom of Information (FOI)
  • Whistle-blower Protection
  • Legal protection of journalists and their sources (including shield laws and contempt)
  • Defamation
  • Intellectual property


You can choose to develop the training material for a jurisdiction you are interested in (e.g., GDPR in the EU; Defamation Law in China; FOI in Australia).

Steps:

  1. Choose: Pick the type of media company, law domain, and the jurisdiction you will work on.
  2. Research: Research the relevant laws in the jurisdiction you have chosen. You can use the assigned readings as a reference; however, you will need to also familiarize yourself with recent case studies, examples, or controversies related to these laws in the media industry. 
  3. Training Materials Development: Determine the format of your training materials. You can produce a booklet, website, infographic, or video. You can also mix the formats. For example, you can create a website with short videos. If you want to design a booklet or an infographic, you can use publishing tools like Adobe InDesign, Canva, Word, Pages, Keynote, or PowerPoint. If you want to produce an interactive web resource, you can use web content management tools such as WordPress, Wix, or Weebly. If you want to produce videos, you can use video editing or animation tools like Adobe Premiere, Final Cut Pro, Adobe Animate. Keep in mind that this is not an essay; regardless of the format, the training material you produce should be visually appealing and professionally formatted.
  4. Prepare Your Training Content: Produce the content of your training materials with the following sections:
  5. Introduction: Provide an overview of the importance of the law area (e.g., privacy, free speech) for the media industry. Explain the potential consequences of non-compliance or organizations and their stakeholders. Please remember that the consequences you are describing should be specific to the law area you selected. For example, if you are focusing on copyright, the consequences you are describing should be about the consequences of failing to comply with copyright laws in the jurisdiction you are focusing on. 
  6. Relevant Laws: Summarize the relevant laws, including their key provisions and what they require from individuals and organizations. Illustrate the application of these laws with examples or scenarios specific to the media industry. Summarize relevant procedures that individuals and organizations should follow to comply with the law. For example, if you are describing the General Data Protection Regulation in the EU, a procedure you may need to describe would be what a company should do if a data subject requests to review the data you have about them. 
  7. Best Practice Guidelines: Develop a set of best practices and ethical guidelines that media professionals should follow to ensure compliance with the relevant laws you are describing. Again, keep in mind that the best practice and ethical guidelines should be specific to the legal area you are focusing on. For example, if you are focusing on defamation laws in Australia, you need to discuss what measures a journalist can take to avoid putting themselves at risk of defaming someone. 


Format and Length

As indicated above, you may submit your piece in whichever format best suits your strengths.

  • If you submit a written document, it should be 1200 words (excluding references, appendices and the AI Table to be appended as part of the assessment submission file).
  • If you submit a multimedia piece (such as a video or podcast), each minute of multimedia is equivalent to 120 words. A 1200-word equivalent is therefore approximately 10 minutes of finished multimedia asset.


Important Notes:

  1. While preparing your training material, please pay attention to formatting and style.
  2. Training materials should be engaging for the reader/viewer/listener.
  3. Training material should assume that the reader has no previous training on the legal issues the training is focusing on. Please use a language that is accessible to adults who do not have prior training in media law. Minimize the use of technical or legal jargon. 
  4. Long texts would confuse and bore the readers. Rather than just relying on lengthy texts, when appropriate, use techniques like bullets, lists. To reduce the burden on the reader, use section headers to divide long texts into short, digestible units. 
  5. Use visual aids to increase the comprehensibility of the information. For example, when you are discussing procedures, you can use flow charts instead of summarizing the procedure with long sentences. Alternatively, you can use text boxes or thought bubbles to draw attention to a key point your readers should remember.
  6. For this assessment, you can use any citation style you use. If you want a style that will minimize interruptions in the text, we recommend the use of a style that relies on footnotes or endnotes (e.g., Chicago Manual of Style 17th edition – full note, IEEE, Springer – Humanities).


Assessment Criteria

The criteria sheet is available on course Blackboard page. 


Use of AI and Machine Translation

AI Policy for Assessment #2: Every instance of AI or MT usage must be clearly referenced, and a completed AI Info Table, accessible on Blackboard, should be attached to summarize their use of AI at the conclusion of the assessment. Students not using AI should use the AI Table to indicate that they have not used AI.
